frontmatter.title: Rudimentary support for RSS feeds in Forester 2.4

mainmatter.p: Each tree generates an RSS feed containing its immediate children; if a tree is located at xxx-NNNN.xml, then the corresponding RSS file will be located at xxx-NNNN.rss.xml. Currently no content is placed in the RSS feed items, but in the future we may add some best-effort plain text linearization of the first paragraph of the tree or something. Feedback on this feature is welcome.
Thanks to Masanori Ogino and Harold Carr for their suggestions which have influenced this release. In addition to the major changes detailed below, there are a few minor improvements:

ul.li: The evaluator for Markdown-style links [title](destination) now allows destination to contain expressions other than text, so long as they evaluate to text.
The XML attribute support introduced in Forester 2.3 has been amended so that attributes appear in square brackets, e.g. \p[class]{myclass}{body}

The forester new command now has a --dest option, which is used to specify the directory in which to deposit the new tree (independently of the --dir option, which specifies the directory to scan for existing trees). Please run forester new --help for details.

mainmatter.p: Each tree generates an RSS feed containing its immediate children; if a tree is located at xxx-NNNN.xml, then the corresponding RSS file will be located at xxx-NNNN.rss.xml. Currently no content is placed in the RSS feed items, but in the future we may add some best-effort plain text linearization of the first paragraph of the tree or something. Feedback on this feature is welcome.

This release adds support for “wikilinks” in the form [[destination]]; this will be rendered as a link whose title is given by the title of the tree at address destination. Users are discouraged from using wikilinks heavily, as it is better for your mind to “work a link into a sentence” naturally; but this can be very useful for linking to biographical trees or daily notes.
This release contains several breaking changes to the XML format that can break custom XSLT stylesheets.

ol.li: Taxa used to be attributes of tree, but they are now child elements of tree/frontmatter. Therefore, what previously was written as <tree taxon="lemma"> <frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </tree> is now written as follows: <tree> <frontmatter> <taxon>lemma</taxon> <!-- ... --> </frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </tree>

li.p: Dates are now stored in machine-readable format. For example, what used to be written <date>August 16, 2023</date> is now written as follows:
It is the responsibility of the XSLT stylesheet to format dates appropriately. Thanks to Masanori Ogino for suggesting this change.

ol.li: All identifiers in the XML format have been regularized to use snake-case; for instance, sourcePath is now source-path and show_heading is now show-heading.

mainmatter.p: Thanks to Masanori Ogino and Harold Carr for their suggestions which have influenced this release. In addition to the major changes detailed below, there are a few minor improvements:

ul.li: The evaluator for Markdown-style links [title](destination) now allows destination to contain expressions other than text, so long as they evaluate to text.
The XML attribute support introduced in Forester 2.3 has been amended so that attributes appear in square brackets, e.g. \p[class]{myclass}{body}

The forester new command now has a --dest option, which is used to specify the directory in which to deposit the new tree (independently of the --dir option, which specifies the directory to scan for existing trees). Please run forester new --help for details.

mainmatter.p: Each tree generates an RSS feed containing its immediate children; if a tree is located at xxx-NNNN.xml, then the corresponding RSS file will be located at xxx-NNNN.rss.xml. Currently no content is placed in the RSS feed items, but in the future we may add some best-effort plain text linearization of the first paragraph of the tree or something. Feedback on this feature is welcome.

This release adds support for “wikilinks” in the form [[destination]]; this will be rendered as a link whose title is given by the title of the tree at address destination. Users are discouraged from using wikilinks heavily, as it is better for your mind to “work a link into a sentence” naturally; but this can be very useful for linking to biographical trees or daily notes.
This release contains several breaking changes to the XML format that can break custom XSLT stylesheets.

ol.li: Taxa used to be attributes of tree, but they are now child elements of tree/frontmatter. Therefore, what previously was written as <tree taxon="lemma"> <frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </tree> is now written as follows: <tree> <frontmatter> <taxon>lemma</taxon> <!-- ... --> </frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </tree>

li.p: Dates are now stored in machine-readable format. For example, what used to be written <date>August 16, 2023</date> is now written as follows:
It is the responsibility of the XSLT stylesheet to format dates appropriately. Thanks to Masanori Ogino for suggesting this change.

ol.li: All identifiers in the XML format have been regularized to use snake-case; for instance, sourcePath is now source-path and show_heading is now show-heading.
